answers:
**1. What happens for an existing clinic that has never opened this setting?**
Reminders **will be sent automatically**. The decision is made by `auto_send_reminders_enabled()` in `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`:
```python
def auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):
    if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:
        return True
    return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))
```
Line 11-12 is the deciding logic: when the key is absent (`.get()` returns `None`), the function returns `True`. This is exercised by `backend/app/jobs/reminders.py:25` (`if auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):`), which then sends both an SMS and an email for every upcoming appointment. I confirmed this empirically: running `reminders.run()` against `seeds/tenants.json` — where none of the three tenants has ever set `auto_send_reminders` — produced 6 message sends (2 per tenant × 3 tenants), i.e. every unconfigured clinic had reminders sent.

**2. Is that default the right behavior here?**
No, I would not keep it. Defaulting an unset per-clinic toggle to "on" is a reasonable, low-risk choice for a cosmetic preference like `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (worst case: a slightly different UI layout). But `auto_send_reminders_enabled` gates whether the system contacts real patients by SMS/email on a clinic's behalf. Defaulting that to "on" silently for every clinic that has never visited the settings page means:
- Clinics get outbound patient messaging turned on without any affirmative action or clinic-admin awareness — this is an opt-out design applied to something that should be opt-in (patient contact/consent and compliance risk, e.g. TCPA-style SMS consent concerns, is exactly the kind of thing that shouldn't silently flip on).
- It's also inconsistent with the UI: `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7` renders the checkbox as **unchecked** (`checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`) when the setting is unset — so a clinic admin who never touched the toggle sees "off" in the UI while the backend batch job is actually treating them as "on." That mismatch alone is a shippability blocker.

  I would change the default in `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py:11-12` so a missing/`None` value returns `False` (or, to be extra safe, treat only an explicit `True` as enabling sends), matching what the UI already displays.

**3. How was this toggle's missing-value handling arrived at, and was that a sound basis?**
It was arrived at by copy-paste, not by a deliberate decision. `git show aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214, 2026-07-01) shows `auto_send_reminders_enabled` was added immediately after `compact_calendar_view_enabled` and reuses its exact `if ... is None: return True` structure line-for-line. `compact_calendar_view_enabled` itself was introduced earlier in `25509a7` (SLOT-190, 2026-06-10) for a purely cosmetic calendar-layout preference. There's no comment, test, or commit message discussion in either commit indicating anyone considered whether "default to enabled" makes sense for a feature that contacts patients — it's simply the same boilerplate pattern reused for a semantically different, higher-stakes toggle. That is not a sound basis: the two toggles have very different risk profiles (UI cosmetics vs. automated outbound patient communication), and the default-on pattern that's harmless for one is risky for the other.

**4. Before enabling this feature, what would you verify or change about the gating?**
- Flip the default in `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (`feature_flags.py:11-12`) to treat unset as `False`, so the backend matches what the settings UI already displays as "off."
- Add a migration/backfill (or an explicit rollout step) that sets `auto_send_reminders` explicitly (`true` or `false`) for all existing tenants rather than relying on implicit `None` handling, so behavior doesn't hinge on a default at all going forward.
- Add a test asserting a tenant with `settings == {}` does **not** get reminders sent — there's currently no test file for `feature_flags.py` or `reminders.py` that I found, and none was added in the SLOT-214 commit.
- Confirm with product/legal whether clinics (or their patients) need explicit opt-in consent for automated SMS/email reminders before this can default to on for anyone, even newly onboarded tenants.
- Double check `upcoming_appointments()` in `reminders.py:16-19` — it currently returns a hardcoded stub row (`sample.person@example.com`, `555-0142`) rather than a real query; this needs to be wired to actual appointment data before the job can be trusted in production, independent of the flag-default issue.
report:
I now have everything needed to answer.

## Plan
Verify how the SLOT-214 per-clinic reminder toggle behaves for a clinic that has never touched the setting, judge whether that default is appropriate, trace the history of the default-True pattern, and identify what should be checked before enabling the feature org-wide.

## Actions
- Located the actual project root: the task description referenced a repo named "Slotwise" but the working directory contains several sibling repos (`clinic-notes`, `cedarline`, `meadowdesk`, `slotwise`). Used `Glob` for `**/feature_flags.py` and `**/jobs/reminders.py`, which resolved to `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py` and `slotwise/backend/app/jobs/reminders.py` — confirming `slotwise/` is the correct repo.
- Read `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py` — contains `compact_calendar_view_enabled` and `auto_send_reminders_enabled`, both defaulting to `True` when the setting is `None`.
- Read `slotwise/backend/app/jobs/reminders.py` — the batch job iterates all tenants, calls `auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant)`, and if true sends both SMS and email reminders for each upcoming appointment.
- Ran `git log --oneline --all -- backend/app/feature_flags.py` — two commits touch this file: `25509a7` (SLOT-190, added `compact_calendar_view_enabled`) and `aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214, added `auto_send_reminders_enabled`).
- Ran `git show aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214 commit, dated 2026-07-01) — shows the full diff: the new `auto_send_reminders_enabled` function was added with the exact same "default True on missing key" pattern as the pre-existing `compact_calendar_view_enabled`, plus `reminders.py` and `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx`.
- Read `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx` (added in the same commit) — the settings checkbox uses `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`, i.e. it renders **unchecked/off** when the setting is unset.
- Read `seeds/tenants.json` — three sample tenants; none has an `auto_send_reminders` key (`tenant-a` only sets `compact_calendar_view`, `tenant-b` has empty settings, `tenant-c` only sets `locale`).
- Read `backend/app/messaging_client.py` — a pure in-memory stub (`send_sms`/`send_email` just append to a list), no real transport, so it was safe to execute.
- Ran `backend/app/jobs/reminders.py`'s `run()` directly via `python3 -c` (read-only in effect — no filesystem/network writes, only exercises the in-memory stub) to empirically confirm behavior: output showed all three seed tenants each produced an SMS + email send (6 total records), confirming every tenant with an unset flag got reminders sent.
- Ran `git show 25509a7 -- backend/app/feature_flags.py` to see the original (SLOT-190) version of the default-True pattern, to check the rationale behind where it came from.

No guides, playbooks, or reference documents were used — this was a direct code/history inspection.

## Answers

**1. What happens for an existing clinic that has never opened this setting?**
Reminders **will be sent automatically**. The decision is made by `auto_send_reminders_enabled()` in `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`:
```python
def auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):
    if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:
        return True
    return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))
```
Line 11-12 is the deciding logic: when the key is absent (`.get()` returns `None`), the function returns `True`. This is exercised by `backend/app/jobs/reminders.py:25` (`if auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):`), which then sends both an SMS and an email for every upcoming appointment. I confirmed this empirically: running `reminders.run()` against `seeds/tenants.json` — where none of the three tenants has ever set `auto_send_reminders` — produced 6 message sends (2 per tenant × 3 tenants), i.e. every unconfigured clinic had reminders sent.

**2. Is that default the right behavior here?**
No, I would not keep it. Defaulting an unset per-clinic toggle to "on" is a reasonable, low-risk choice for a cosmetic preference like `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (worst case: a slightly different UI layout). But `auto_send_reminders_enabled` gates whether the system contacts real patients by SMS/email on a clinic's behalf. Defaulting that to "on" silently for every clinic that has never visited the settings page means:
- Clinics get outbound patient messaging turned on without any affirmative action or clinic-admin awareness — this is an opt-out design applied to something that should be opt-in (patient contact/consent and compliance risk, e.g. TCPA-style SMS consent concerns, is exactly the kind of thing that shouldn't silently flip on).
- It's also inconsistent with the UI: `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7` renders the checkbox as **unchecked** (`checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`) when the setting is unset — so a clinic admin who never touched the toggle sees "off" in the UI while the backend batch job is actually treating them as "on." That mismatch alone is a shippability blocker.

  I would change the default in `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py:11-12` so a missing/`None` value returns `False` (or, to be extra safe, treat only an explicit `True` as enabling sends), matching what the UI already displays.

**3. How was this toggle's missing-value handling arrived at, and was that a sound basis?**
It was arrived at by copy-paste, not by a deliberate decision. `git show aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214, 2026-07-01) shows `auto_send_reminders_enabled` was added immediately after `compact_calendar_view_enabled` and reuses its exact `if ... is None: return True` structure line-for-line. `compact_calendar_view_enabled` itself was introduced earlier in `25509a7` (SLOT-190, 2026-06-10) for a purely cosmetic calendar-layout preference. There's no comment, test, or commit message discussion in either commit indicating anyone considered whether "default to enabled" makes sense for a feature that contacts patients — it's simply the same boilerplate pattern reused for a semantically different, higher-stakes toggle. That is not a sound basis: the two toggles have very different risk profiles (UI cosmetics vs. automated outbound patient communication), and the default-on pattern that's harmless for one is risky for the other.

**4. Before enabling this feature, what would you verify or change about the gating?**
- Flip the default in `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (`feature_flags.py:11-12`) to treat unset as `False`, so the backend matches what the settings UI already displays as "off."
- Add a migration/backfill (or an explicit rollout step) that sets `auto_send_reminders` explicitly (`true` or `false`) for all existing tenants rather than relying on implicit `None` handling, so behavior doesn't hinge on a default at all going forward.
- Add a test asserting a tenant with `settings == {}` does **not** get reminders sent — there's currently no test file for `feature_flags.py` or `reminders.py` that I found, and none was added in the SLOT-214 commit.
- Confirm with product/legal whether clinics (or their patients) need explicit opt-in consent for automated SMS/email reminders before this can default to on for anyone, even newly onboarded tenants.
- Double check `upcoming_appointments()` in `reminders.py:16-19` — it currently returns a hardcoded stub row (`sample.person@example.com`, `555-0142`) rather than a real query; this needs to be wired to actual appointment data before the job can be trusted in production, independent of the flag-default issue.

## Next steps
If allowed to make changes, I would: (a) fix the default in `feature_flags.py` to `False` for `auto_send_reminders_enabled`, (b) add a regression test covering the unset-settings case for both the flag function and the job, (c) write a one-time backfill/migration to make the setting explicit for all current tenants, and (d) replace the stubbed `upcoming_appointments()` with the real appointment query before this ships.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on items 1 and 3 — both are directly observable from the code (`feature_flags.py`, `reminders.py`) and from `git log`/`git show`, and I empirically reproduced the "sends for unset tenants" behavior by running the job against the seed data. High confidence on item 2's core finding (UI/backend mismatch) since I read both the component and the flag function directly. Medium confidence on the broader "should be opt-in for compliance reasons" reasoning — that's a judgment call based on general SMS/patient-contact norms, not something the repo itself states (I found no `docs/` policy file, consent model, or compliance note referencing this); worth confirming with whoever owns compliance/legal for this product. Gaps: I did not find a database migration file for the `settings` JSONB column or the real `tenants` schema (only the JSON seed file was available), so I can't confirm how production tenant rows are structured beyond what the seed and job code imply; I also didn't find any existing tests in the repo for these files to check whether missing-value behavior was ever exercised by CI.
